points: A figure has line symmetry if a line (axis of symmetry) divides it into two mirror halves that perfectly overlap when folded.
A square has 4 lines of symmetry, while a rectangle has only 2; a circle is uniquely symmetric with infinite axes along its diameters.
Reflection symmetry maps every point on one side of the axis to a corresponding point on the other side at an equal distance.
Symmetrical patterns can be created using techniques like ink blots, paper folding, and decorative paper cutting.
The punching game illustrates that holes made in folded paper appear in symmetrical positions relative to the fold lines when unfolded.
Completing symmetric drawings on squared or dot paper involves mirroring each point across a given axis of symmetry.
A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks identical after being rotated about a fixed centre by an angle less than $360^\circ$.
The order of rotational symmetry is the number of times a figure coincides with itself during a complete $360^\circ$ turn.
For a figure to have rotational symmetry, its smallest angle of rotation must be a factor of the total $360^\circ$ in a full turn.
Regular polygons (equilateral triangle, square, hexagon) have an equal number of side counts, lines of symmetry, and rotational orders.
Circles are the most symmetrical shapes, possessing both infinite lines of reflection and infinite angles of rotational symmetry.
Symbolic structures like the Ashoka Chakra (24 spokes) demonstrate both 24 lines of symmetry and 24 rotational angles of $15^\circ$ each.
